questionText: In Texas, a towing contractor orally agreed with several subcontractor towing companies that, for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract, the subcontractors would provide towing and storage services and in return would receive a share of the work or related benefits. No signed writing was ever made. The contractor later argued that the arrangement could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early. Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
In Texas, an oral contract is generally enforceable if there is (1) mutual assent (offer and acceptance), (2) consideration, and (3) reasonably definite terms. The main obstacle here is the Texas statute of frauds—because the alleged term is “for the five-year term” of the government towing contract and there is no signed writing.

1) Statute of frauds (Texas one-year rule)
Texas requires a writing for a contract “that is not to be performed within one year” from the date it is made. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).

On the face of the description (“for the five-year term”), a court would initially suspect the arrangement is within the statute of frauds because it is a multi-year deal that would normally extend beyond one year. However, Texas looks to whether the contract, by its terms, is capable of being performed within one year—not whether that performance is likely.

So the key issue is how the subcontractors’ obligations were tied to the government contract’s duration:

- If the parties’ oral agreement meant “the subcontractors will provide services for as long as the government towing contract lasts” (i.e., it automatically ends when the government program ends), then the agreement is capable of being fully performed within one year if the government program terminates within the first year. Under that reading, the agreement can fall outside the statute of frauds even though it is described as a “five-year” term—because the contract could end earlier without breach.
- If instead the oral agreement guaranteed that the subcontractors would perform for the full five years regardless of whether the government program ended early (for example, termination of the government program would not shorten the subcontractors’ contract term), then the contract would not be performable within one year and would fall squarely within § 26.01(b)(6), making the oral agreement unenforceable absent a writing (or another narrow exception).

Your prompt frames the contractor’s defense as: the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early.” That is consistent with the first interpretation (a duration tied to the government contract). If the subcontractors’ performance and the contractor’s obligation to provide work/share were coextensive with the government contract’s continued existence, then the agreement is likely capable of being performed within a year and therefore not barred by the statute of frauds.

2) Other enforceability considerations (definiteness and proof)
Even if the statute of frauds does not bar enforcement, the subcontractors still must prove an enforceable agreement with sufficiently definite terms. Here, the compensation is described as “a share of the work or related benefits.” That can be enforceable if the parties had a workable understanding (e.g., a percentage, a method for determining the share, or a course of dealing/accounting that shows how the “share” was calculated). If the compensation mechanism was entirely unspecified such that the court cannot determine what each subcontractor was owed, a court may find the agreement too indefinite. But actual performance under the arrangement usually helps demonstrate (and quantify) the parties’ intended allocation.

3) If the statute of frauds does apply
If the court concludes the agreement truly required performance for five years regardless of early termination, then the oral contract claim is likely barred by § 26.01(b)(6) because there is no signed writing.

Even then, the subcontractors may still be able to recover under non-contract theories such as restitution/quantum meruit for the reasonable value of services the contractor accepted (Texas often allows recovery for the value of benefits retained, even when the underlying contract is unenforceable under the statute of frauds). But that is different from enforcing the contract’s agreed “share” terms.

Conclusion
The agreement is most likely enforceable if the oral deal was effectively “for the duration of the government towing contract,” meaning it could end within one year if the government program ended within that first-year period. In that case, the one-year statute-of-frauds writing requirement would likely not apply, and the subcontractors can pursue enforcement (subject to proving the essential terms, especially how the “share” was determined).

If, instead, the oral agreement guaranteed a fixed five-year subcontract term independent of early termination of the government program, then the arrangement is likely unenforceable under Texas’s statute of frauds absent a writing, though the subcontractors may still pursue recovery for the value of services actually provided.

Under Texas law, an oral contract is generally enforceable if it has mutual assent and reasonably definite terms, and if consideration supports the bargain. The main potential bar here is the Texas Statute of Frauds for agreements that “are not to be performed within one year from the date of making the agreement.” Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6). No signed writing is present, so the key question becomes whether the agreement was, “by its terms,” incapable of full performance within one year.

1) Formation / consideration / definiteness
Based on your facts, there appears to be consideration: the subcontractors would provide towing and storage services, and the contractor would allow them to receive a share of the work or related benefits. If the parties agreed on the scope of services and the term (tied to the government towing contract) and the “share” is ascertainable (e.g., by an agreed percentage/formula, or by objective criteria the parties used in practice), a Texas court can find enough definiteness to enforce an oral services contract.

If, however, the “share of the work or related benefits” is too vague to determine what the subcontractors were promised (no percentage, no method, no objective basis), the agreement could be attacked for indefiniteness even if the Statute of Frauds does not apply. Your prompt doesn’t give enough detail to decide that issue conclusively.

2) Statute of Frauds (one-year clause) is the central issue
The parties’ stated “five-year term” would normally trigger § 26.01(b)(6) because a five-year performance period is more than one year.

But Texas applies the “possibility of performance” test: the Statute of Frauds applies only if the contract, by its terms, cannot possibly be fully performed within one year—not if performance merely would likely take longer. So an agreement is typically outside the Statute of Frauds if it is capable of being fully performed within one year because of an early-termination contingency.

Here, the arrangement is described as lasting “for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract.” That phrasing matters. If the subcontractor arrangement was coextensive with the government contract—i.e., it automatically ended when the government towing contract ended—then the subcontract agreement could have been fully performed in less than a year if the government contract terminated early (for example, for convenience or another allowable early-termination ground under the government contract). In that circumstance, the contract is “capable of performance within one year,” and the Statute of Frauds would not bar enforcement even though the expected duration was five years.

On the other hand, if the subcontractor agreement guaranteed five years of towing/storage regardless of whether the government program ended early (or if early termination was purely speculative and not part of the agreement’s structure), then the agreement would be “not to be performed within one year” and § 26.01(b)(6) would require a signed writing.

Your facts suggest the contractor will argue: “the arrangement could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early.” That is exactly the kind of argument Texas courts evaluate under the “could possibly be performed within one year” framework. Whether it succeeds will likely depend on what the oral agreement actually meant (coextensive duration vs. fixed five-year commitment) and what early termination rights existed under the government contract that were part of the parties’ contemplation.

3) Even if unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, restitution may still be available
If a court finds the oral agreement is within the one-year Statute of Frauds and unenforceable for lack of a writing, that does not necessarily leave the subcontractors with zero remedies. Texas law commonly allows recovery in restitution/quantum meruit for the reasonable value of services actually provided and benefits received by the contractor, even when the contract itself cannot be enforced. That recovery typically does not enforce the contract’s promised “share,” but it can provide compensation for what was actually delivered.

4) Likely outcome on your facts
On the information given, the strongest legal reason to find enforceability is that the subcontractors’ obligations were tied to the government towing contract’s term, and thus the arrangement could have ended within a year if the government contract terminated early. If the court determines that early termination of the government contract was a possibility at the time the parties formed the oral arrangement and that the subcontract agreement would end when the government contract ended, then the Statute of Frauds likely does not apply, making the agreement potentially enforceable (assuming the “share” and other key terms are sufficiently definite).

If, instead, the agreement was effectively a fixed five-year commitment independent of early termination, then it is very likely barred by § 26.01(b)(6) absent a signed writing, with subcontractors’ best fallback being restitution/quantum meruit for services performed.

If you tell me whether the government towing contract could be terminated early (e.g., for convenience) and whether the oral subcontract arrangement expressly or practically ended when the government program ended, I can narrow the likely Statute of Frauds outcome and the strength of any “share of work” claim.

Yes—on the facts given, the arrangement is likely enforceable, despite the lack of a signed writing, because the key Texas statute-of-frauds issue turns on whether the agreement was capable of being fully performed within one year.

1) Enforceability of oral contracts in Texas generally
Texas recognizes oral contracts if:
- there is mutual assent (agreement on the essential terms),
- consideration exists (each side promised something of value),
- and the agreement’s terms are sufficiently definite for a court to determine what was promised and to fashion a remedy.

Here, the subcontractors promised to provide towing and storage services, and the contractor promised that the subcontractors would receive a “share of the work or related benefits” during the term of the government towing contract. That is classic consideration on both sides. The main remaining challenge is whether any “writing required” rule applies, and whether the “share” is too indefinite. Based on the prompt, the share mechanism was sufficiently agreed upon to constitute enforceable terms (courts often determine the economics from the parties’ understanding and course of dealing, even if the percentage/formula is not in a signed writing).

2) The statute of frauds issue: Texas Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(a)(1)
Texas requires a writing for certain agreements, including:
- agreements “that are not to be performed within one year from the date of making.”

A five-year term, standing alone, often triggers this rule. But Texas courts focus on a different point: whether the contract was capable of being performed within one year—not whether it would likely last longer.

So the question becomes: could this subcontract agreement end (i.e., be fully performed) within a year?

3) Contractor’s argument (“it could have ended sooner if the government program was terminated early”) matters
The contractor is basically invoking the “capable of performance within one year” doctrine. If the arrangement was tied to the government’s towing contract term and the government could terminate early at any time, then the subcontract could also end within one year. Under Texas law, that possibility is often enough to avoid the one-year statute-of-frauds bar.

On your facts, the subcontractors’ obligation is described as lasting “for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract.” That strongly suggests the parties intended the subcontract to track the prime contract’s duration. If the government terminated early, the prime contract ends early, and the basis for the subcontract ends early as well—meaning full performance could occur within one year.

Importantly, it doesn’t have to be guaranteed; it just has to be possible from the outset. A government’s unilateral termination right (or other event outside the parties’ control) typically provides that “possibility.”

4) When the statute of frauds would bar enforcement (contrast)
If, instead, the oral agreement had bound the subcontractors to provide services for a fixed five-year period regardless of whether the government contract ended early (for example, the subcontractors were committed for five years even if there was no government work), then the agreement would not be “capable of performance” within one year and the statute of frauds would likely apply.

Also, if the agreement required performance beyond one year and early termination was only something the parties could choose later after one year (rather than a contingency that could happen immediately), that can lead to a statute-of-frauds problem. But your facts point the other way: the five-year “term” is explicitly linked to the government contract.

5) Conclusion
Given that:
- the agreement is an ordinary services/benefit-sharing arrangement (no special writing requirement appears on those facts beyond the one-year rule),
- the only plausible writing issue is the one-year statute of frauds, and
- the agreement was expressly tied to the government contract’s five-year term but could end earlier if the government program terminated,

the agreement is likely enforceable in Texas because it was capable of being fully performed within one year if the government terminated early. The contractor’s argument supports that outcome rather than defeats it.

Under Texas law, an oral contract is generally enforceable if there is (1) mutual assent, (2) reasonably certain terms, and (3) consideration. The absence of a written document is not automatically fatal. The main potential obstacle here is the Texas statute of frauds for certain agreements “not to be performed within one year.”

1) Statute of frauds (one-year rule)
Texas applies the “capacity to be performed” test, not the parties’ expectations. An oral agreement is within the one-year statute of frauds only if, by its terms, it is impossible for the contract to be fully performed within one year after it is made. If full performance is objectively possible within one year—because of an event that could occur even if unlikely—then the statute of frauds does not bar enforcement.

Here, the parties orally agreed that the subcontractors would provide towing/storage services for the “five-year term” of the contractor’s government towing contract. The contractor later argues the arrangement could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early. That argument matters because if early termination of the underlying government contract would end the subcontractor arrangement as well (i.e., there would be nothing left for the subcontractors to perform after termination), then full performance of the subcontractors’ obligations could occur within one year if the government program terminated within that year. Under Texas’s possibility test, that can take the agreement out of the statute of frauds even if the contract is expected to last five years.

So, assuming the oral agreement was truly contingent on the life of the government towing contract (i.e., it ended when the government program ended), the fact that the government could terminate early means the subcontractor agreement was capable of being fully performed within one year. In that circumstance, the “no writing” statute-of-frauds defense would likely fail.

2) What would change the result
If, despite being tied to the government contract “term,” the parties’ oral agreement actually required the contractor to provide (or the subcontractors to remain obligated to provide) the benefits/services for a fixed five-year period regardless of whether the government terminated early—e.g., the subcontractors had a guaranteed five-year opportunity, or they were obligated to stand ready/pay costs for five years even if no government work existed—then full performance would be impossible within one year. In that case, the agreement would fall within the one-year statute of frauds and would generally be unenforceable without a writing (absent a specific exception such as an applicable equitable doctrine recognized under Texas law or admission in pleadings).

3) Certainty of the bargain (separate from statute of frauds)
Even if the agreement avoids the statute of frauds, Texas still requires the essential terms to be sufficiently definite to enforce. The prompt describes the consideration as a “share of the work or related benefits.” That phrase can be enforceable if the parties had a workable, reasonably certain understanding of how the “share” would be calculated or allocated (for example, a defined percentage, a rotation system, geographic assignments, or a method tied to dispatch/volume). If the “share” was too indefinite—such that a court would have to guess fundamental business terms—Texas courts may refuse to enforce or may limit relief.

However, if the subcontractors performed and the contractor accepted the services and operated in a manner consistent with the “share” arrangement (e.g., subcontractors were consistently routed work and/or received agreed ancillary benefits), that performance and course of dealing can help establish that the parties had an enforceable agreement with sufficiently definite terms.

4) Practical bottom line
Based on the facts given, the contractor’s “it could have ended sooner” argument is aligned with how Texas treats the one-year statute of frauds. If the agreement was intended to run only for as long as the government towing contract was in effect—and therefore could terminate within a year through the government’s early termination—then the oral agreement is likely not barred by the statute of frauds and would be potentially enforceable, subject to proof that the “share of work/benefits” terms were reasonably certain (and/or evidenced by the parties’ actual performance).

If you want, tell me whether (a) the government’s early termination would automatically end the subcontractors’ obligations, and (b) whether the subcontractors’ “share” was based on a percentage, rotation, territory, or some other measurable allocation. Those details are usually decisive for both the statute-of-frauds and definiteness issues.

In Texas, oral agreements can be enforceable, but the enforceability of an oral “five-year term” arrangement is mainly limited by Texas’s Statute of Frauds (SOF).

1) Does the oral agreement fall within Texas’s Statute of Frauds?
Texas requires a writing for “an agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the date of making” the agreement. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).

On the face of the deal described, the parties contemplated a five-year term (the term of the contractor’s government towing contract), which would ordinarily trigger the SOF because it is not “capable of being performed” within one year.

However, Texas applies the SOF based on whether the contract, by its terms, is capable of full performance within one year—not whether it will likely take longer. The key question is whether the arrangement could end (and thus be fully performed) within one year if the government program terminated early.

Here, the subcontractors’ right/obligation is expressly tied to the contractor’s government towing contract “for the five-year term.” If that government contract could be terminated early, then the towing/storage arrangement would also end early (because its underlying term no longer exists). That means the agreement could be fully performed within one year in the event of an early termination.

So, if early termination of the government towing contract is legally possible (for example, the government had a right to terminate for convenience or otherwise could end it at any time), then the oral subcontract arrangement is arguably “capable of performance within one year,” and therefore not barred by § 26.01(b)(6) despite the five-year label.

If, instead, the government program is not terminable early as a matter of the government contract (i.e., it truly runs for five years with no early termination right), then the arrangement by its terms cannot be fully performed within one year, and the SOF would bar enforcement absent a writing.

2) What about the contractor’s argument that it could have ended sooner?
The contractor’s argument (“it could have ended sooner if the government program was terminated early”) matters in two ways:

- For SOF purposes, it supports the subcontractors’ position: if early termination was possible, the agreement could be fully performed within one year and therefore may fall outside the SOF.
- On the merits, it goes to the scope/termination of the obligation: if the government contract ended early, then the parties’ subcontract obligations would end when the underlying government contract ended (assuming the oral deal truly was coextensive with that term).

So the contractor’s “could end sooner” point does not automatically defeat enforceability; it may actually prevent the SOF from applying, and it may also limit liability if the government termination occurred.

3) Are the contract terms sufficiently definite for enforcement?
Even if the SOF does not apply, the subcontractors still have to prove an enforceable contract: mutual assent plus consideration, and sufficiently definite terms.

Based on your facts:
- Scope/work: providing towing and storage services.
- Timing: during the term of the contractor’s government towing contract.
- Compensation: a “share of the work or related benefits.”

A “share of the work” arrangement can be enforceable if the parties agreed to some mechanism for allocating or valuing the work/benefits (even if not perfectly quantified in writing). Texas courts often enforce contracts where the essential terms are agreed and any remaining uncertainty can be resolved with reasonable inferences or by the parties’ performance. But if the “share” is so vague that the court cannot determine what the subcontractors were entitled to in any meaningful way, damages could be difficult and the claim might shift toward restitution/quantum meruit for value actually received.

4) Bottom-line conclusion
- If early termination of the underlying government towing contract was legally possible, the oral subcontract agreement is likely enforceable because it was capable of full performance within one year, taking it outside the Statute of Frauds. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 26.01(b)(6).
- If early termination was not actually possible under the government contract (i.e., performance could not occur within one year), the oral agreement would likely be unenforceable under the SOF absent a writing.
- Separately, even if enforceable, the subcontractors still must show the “share of the work/benefits” was definite enough (or can be determined from performance) to support damages; otherwise recovery may be limited or proceed under an equitable/restition theory rather than strict contract damages.

Yes—an oral agreement like this is likely enforceable in Texas, but its enforceability would be tied to the same contingency and duration as the contractor’s government towing contract, not a guaranteed fixed commitment regardless of early termination.

1) Oral contracts are generally enforceable; the key issue is the Texas statute of frauds
Texas generally allows contracts to be formed orally. The main potential bar is the Texas statute of frauds for agreements that “cannot be performed” within one year.

Texas Business & Commerce Code § 26.01(b)(6) requires a writing if, by the terms of the agreement, performance is not possible within one year from when the contract is made.

Texas applies a “could be performed within one year” approach: if the contract, as written/understood, is capable of being fully performed within one year, the statute of frauds does not apply—even if the parties expected it would run longer.

2) The five-year term does not automatically make it unenforceable because the government contract could end early
Here, the parties’ promise was: subcontractors would provide towing and storage services “for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract,” in exchange for a share of work or related benefits.

That matters because the subcontractors’ performance obligation was linked to the duration of the contractor’s government contract. If the government can terminate early (as the contractor argues), then the entire arrangement could end sooner, meaning the agreement is capable of being fully performed within one year in the contingency where the government terminates early.

So the contractor’s argument (“the arrangement could have ended sooner if the government program terminated early”) is typically the kind of fact that supports enforceability against a statute-of-frauds defense, not defeats it. In other words:
- The agreement is not barred merely because the expected duration was five years.
- Because early termination is a real possibility contemplated by the structure of the deal (performance is coextensive with the government contract term), it is not “incapable” of being performed within one year.

3) The “early termination” argument goes to scope/duration, not whether the contract exists
Even if enforceable, the contractor would usually be able to argue that:
- the subcontractors’ obligations lasted only for as long as the government contract (i.e., if the government program ended early, the subcontractors’ duty to provide services would end at that point), and
- the contractor is not liable for refusing to provide continued work beyond the termination event, because the subcontractor’s performance was tied to that government contract term.

But that is different from saying the whole oral agreement is unenforceable. At most, it limits damages to the period the contingency allowed/required performance.

4) Other enforceability problems are possible (but depend on the missing facts)
Even if the statute of frauds does not apply, enforcement can still fail if the agreement is too indefinite. In Texas, a contract must be sufficiently definite about essential terms.

Potential indefiniteness issues here would include:
- what “share of the work” means in measurable terms (e.g., fixed percentage, specific allocation method, or a defined formula), and
- how “related benefits” were calculated.

If the parties’ oral understandings provide an objective method for determining the share/benefits (for example, a percentage of jobs actually assigned, or an agreed pricing/commission structure), that generally supports enforceability. If not, the contractor could argue the deal is too indefinite to enforce beyond restitution/quantum meruit (and even that would require the subcontractors to have actually performed and conferred value).

5) Bottom line
Likely enforceable. The oral agreement is probably not barred by the statute of frauds because it was structured so that performance could end within one year if the government towing program terminated early. The more practical effect of the contractor’s “early termination” point is to limit the subcontractors’ recoverable period and obligations to the duration of the government contract, not to eliminate enforceability altogether.

Those details strongly affect both the statute-of-frauds analysis and the “indefiniteness” risk.

Under Texas law, an oral contract is generally enforceable unless a statute of frauds or another legal bar applies. The key issue here is Texas’s one-year statute of frauds.

1. Statute of frauds issue (Texas Business & Commerce Code § 26.01(b)(6))
Texas will not enforce “any agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the date of making” unless there is a signed writing.

Texas applies a “by its terms” test: the question is whether the contract, as written/orally agreed, is incapable of full performance within one year—i.e., whether it necessarily requires performance that extends beyond one year. If the contract could, by its own terms, be fully performed within one year, then the statute of frauds does not apply even if it ends up lasting longer in practice.

2. Applying that test to the towing-subcontractor arrangement
You describe an oral arrangement tied to “the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract,” under which subcontractors would provide towing/storage and receive a share of work/related benefits.

Two factual possibilities matter:

A. If the subcontractors’ obligations were truly “for five years” in an unconditional way
If, notwithstanding any early termination of the government program, the parties’ agreement required the subcontractors to keep providing services and/or required the contractor to provide the promised benefit-sharing for the full five-year period, then the agreement would necessarily not be fully performed within one year. In that case, the oral agreement would fall squarely within § 26.01(b)(6) and would be unenforceable absent a writing.

B. If the agreement was coextensive with the government contract and could end early
The contractor’s argument—that the arrangement “could have ended sooner if the government towing program was terminated early”—is relevant to the “capable of performance” test. If, by the parties’ agreement, the subcontractors’ duty to provide towing/storage (and/or their right to the share of work/benefits) would terminate when the government program ended, then the contract could be fully “performed” within one year if the government terminated within that first year. Under Texas law, that potential is enough to take the oral agreement outside the one-year statute of frauds, because the contract would not be incapable of performance within one year “by its terms.”

So, if the subcontractors can prove the arrangement was effectively conditional on the government contract continuing (i.e., early termination ended the subcontractors’ performance and compensation obligations), the agreement is likely enforceable despite being oral.

3. Burden and practical consequences
Because statute of frauds is an affirmative defense, the contractor generally bears the burden of establishing that the oral agreement is within § 26.01(b)(6).

Also, even if a court were to decide the oral agreement is unenforceable under the statute of frauds, Texas still typically allows recovery under restitution theories (quantum meruit/unjust enrichment) for services actually provided and accepted—so the subcontractors may still recover the reasonable value of towing/storage performed, though they may be barred from enforcing the contract’s terms as such.

4. Bottom-line conclusion
- If the oral agreement required performance for the full five-year term regardless of early termination of the government program, then it is likely unenforceable under Texas’s one-year statute of frauds.
- If, however, the parties’ agreement made the arrangement terminate when the government towing program terminated (so that full performance could occur within one year if the government ended the program within a year), then the contract is likely enforceable because it is not “incapable of performance within one year” by its terms.

In Texas, an oral contract is generally enforceable if it has (1) an offer and acceptance (mutual assent), (2) consideration, and (3) sufficiently definite terms for a court to determine what each side must do and what is owed. The key obstacle to enforcement here is likely the Statute of Frauds—specifically Texas’s “one-year rule.”

1) Statute of Frauds (one-year rule)
Texas requires a signed writing for “an agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the date of making.” A contract falls within (b)(6) only if, by its terms, it cannot possibly be fully performed within one year. Texas courts focus on what is possible under the contract as agreed at the time of formation, not on what is most likely or what the parties expected to happen.

Here, the subcontractors’ role was “for the five-year term of the contractor’s government towing contract,” and the subcontractors would provide towing/storage and receive a share of the work/related benefits. The contractor argues the arrangement “could have ended sooner” if the government program terminated early.

That matters legally because it cuts directly against the one-year rule. If the subcontractors’ obligations were coextensive with the existence of the government towing contract—i.e., when the government contract ends early, there is no further obligation for towing/storage and thus the agreement is fully performed/ended—then the contract could be completely performed within one year if the government terminated within the first year. Under Texas law, the existence of that possibility is enough to take the agreement out of the one-year Statute of Frauds, even if the agreement’s nominal duration was five years.

So, as framed by the facts (performance tied to the government towing contract’s duration, and termination of that program would end the arrangement), the agreement is likely not barred by the one-year Statute of Frauds.

2) The contractor’s “could have ended sooner” argument
Interestingly, the contractor is using the possibility of early termination as a shield, but under the one-year-rule analysis, that same possibility generally helps the subcontractors. The Statute of Frauds defense typically fails if the contract could have been performed within a year (even if it usually wouldn’t). If early termination would stop the subcontractors’ duties and resolve the agreement within a year, then the writing requirement should not apply.

3) If the court construes the deal as a fixed five-year obligation regardless of termination
There is a potential counterpoint: if the oral agreement actually obligated the subcontractors to remain available and perform (or otherwise bear obligations) for the full five-year term even when the government program ended early, then the contract would not be capable of full performance within one year. In that event, the one-year rule would likely apply, making the oral agreement unenforceable as a contract (though the subcontractors might still pursue restitution/quantum meruit for the value of services actually provided).

On your facts, however, the agreement appears to be tied to the government program’s term, and the contractor’s own argument that the arrangement could end early suggests it was not intended to require five years of performance independent of the government contract.

4) Other enforceability considerations: definiteness and consideration
Assuming mutual assent and consideration are shown, the remaining question is whether the “share of the work or related benefits” is sufficiently definite to enforce. Texas does not require perfect precision, but the terms must be ascertainable enough for a court to determine obligations and compute damages. In practice, courts will sometimes infer or determine the measure of a “share” from the surrounding deal structure (e.g., the government contract’s scope/pricing and the parties’ course of dealing). If the share was completely indeterminate, that could be a separate basis for a failure of enforceability, but that issue is distinct from the Statute of Frauds.

Conclusion
Most likely, the oral agreement is enforceable in Texas because it is plausibly capable of being fully performed within one year if the government towing program ended early—meaning it falls outside the one-year Statute of Frauds. The subcontractors would still need to prove the contract terms with enough definiteness to show what they were promised and what the contractor promised in return, but the “no writing” defense should generally not defeat the claim on these facts.

If instead the evidence shows the subcontractors were obligated for a fixed five-year performance regardless of early government termination, then the oral contract would likely be barred by the one-year Statute of Frauds, limiting recovery to alternative remedies such as quantum meruit for services actually provided.
